I think it has something to do with Sweden not experiencing a big war or political upheaval for centuries Even if you suppress income equality to minimum if there is a continuous accumulation of wealth through generations there will high wealth inequality Also Sweden has relatively low inheritance tax which maximizes generational accumulation Korea Japan which have high inheritance tax being relatively low on wealth inequality support that theory

While there is definitely truth to the inheritance tax explanation these sort of comparisons usually ignore pensions which heavily distorts the picture the bigger the welfare state is Your regular Swede probably relies mainly on their pension accounts not on personal wealth whereas people in countries with a weaker welfare system invest heavily in housing and other assets that are included in the statistics Sweden is also popular for being the first European country that introduced something similar to an IRA401k based on stocks but with a mandatory countrymanaged option for everyone which is basically a lowcost index fund It has been highly successful but if those accounts are not represented as wealth it definitely drags down the median

Also I follow Scandinavian politics very very closely am fairly knowledgeable about it as well Id say it was through the Scandinavian party systems that I first realized the extent to which class still plays a huge role in Scandinavian society to this day One of the most fundamental characteristics about the party systems of all three of these countries is how profoundly installed it is in the collective imagery the psyche of Danes Swedes Norwegians the notion that all the political parties that happen to belong to their respective party systems can be sorted into either one or the other of two fundamentally distinct ideologically atodds blocs that divide the political spectrum completely in two in all three of these countries A socialistredredgreen bloc encompassing the Democratic Socialists both formerly MarxistLeninist communist nonformerly MarxistLeninist communist ones the Greens the Social Democrats in Denmarks instance also the Social LiberalsRadicals since 1990 in Norways instance also the Nordic Agrarian Centrists since 2005 in Swedens instance also the increasingly more more oriented towards hugely culturally liberal centrist urbanites who are rejecting the Liberals for dropping the cordon sanitaire rather than towards the actual rural folk that used to be the partys main pretty much only base as the Nordic Agrarian party of the country Nordic Agrarian Centrists since 2022 A bourgeoiscivicnonsocialistblue bloc basically encompassing everything to the right of the socialistredredgreen one namely more specifically the Liberals who in Denmarks instance actually come originally from the political tradition of Nordic Agrarian Centrists instead but who today are way more of a Liberal party than of a Nordic Agrarian Centrist one the Christian Democrats the Conservatives the RightLibertarians as well as given how all bourgeois parties in all three countries already have either left the bourgeois bloc for good as well as for all intents purposes pretty much joined the socialistredredgreen one as well as a result precisely in order to maintain their resolutely unyielding utter opposition to dropping the cordon sanitaire as well or completely explicitly agreed openly in public to drop the cordon sanitaire also the Nativist Populists including the literal former neonazi ones who priorly had always been excluded from the bourgeois bloc but whom since the cordon sanitaire has been dropped have been pretty much entirely embraced as bourgeois Even in Denmarks instance where unlike in Sweden Norway sometimes like in the current probably next cabinets instance for example coalitions are formed at the national level which do not strictly align with this bloc divide are intrabloc coalitions instead the blocs still very much remain hugely present arent considered something of the past in any way whatsoever while this doesnt apply anywhere near as much to the electorate of the Christian Democrats which seems to be way less affluent on average than that of the Liberals the Conservatives nor of the Nordic Agrarian Centrists which anyways as Ive said in Norways instance joined the socialistredredgreen bloc back in 2005 in Swedens in 2022 therefore only remaining within the bourgeois one in Denmark where as previously said they are way more of a Liberal than of a Nordic Agrarian Centrist party anyway certainly doesnt apply at all whatsoever to the electorate of the Nativist Populists which to a large extent consists on some of the least affluent most bluecollar sectors of Scandinavian society when it comes to the electorate of all the rest of the bourgeois bloc though namely the Liberals the Conservatives Id argue classwise this electorate is indeed very much bourgeois in the sense that its virtually completely circumscribed to the relatively to substantially quite affluent middle classes uppermiddle classes Affluent middle classes uppermiddle classes with either Liberal or Conservative political inclinations for whom I reckon belonging to a completely separate social political group than the bluecollar workingclasslowermiddle class that historically has overwhelmingly backed the socialistredredgreen bloc rejected the bourgeois one but which is now increasingly trending towards the direction of the Nativist Populists therefore given how these have now unambiguously joined the bloc also increasingly trending in a way towards the direction of the bourgeois bloc as well is a pretty major component of how they view the position their segment of society occupies within it vs the one of other segments of society who occupy an entirely different one a bluecollar workingclasslowermiddle class one

Out of all the ones that have been proposed as prototypical models of welfare regime subtypes virtually acrosstheboard adoption attestation as such within the field remains something only five of them have succeeded thus far in gathering These are The Social Democratic one Sweden Norway Denmark Finland Iceland The ConservativeCorporatist one Germany Austria Switzerland Luxembourg the Netherlands Belgium France The Liberal one the UK Ireland the US Canada Australia New Zealand The Southern EuropeanMediterranean one Portugal Spain Italy Malta Greece Cyprus The ProductivistDevelopmental one Japan South Korea Taiwan Singapore China Hong Kong Macao Obviously these are only a fraction of the countries of the world various welfare regime subtypes have been further proposed to group other countries a number of them gathering the recognition of pretty large segments of the field but still not yet to an extent that would warrant their addition alongside with the Social Democratic the ConservativeCorporatist the Liberal the Southern EuropeanMediterranean the ProductivistDevelopmental welfare regime subtypes as additional categories ratified by the current paradigm in the field In any case this is all to say in my view the empirical evidence that the first one the Social Democratic one is by far the one out of the five leading to the best outcomes out of all of them by a mile in fact is simply undisputably irrefutable its simply a neutral fact that it is I highly struggle to understand why would anyone favour one of the other four welfare regimes over it advocate for it rather than for the Social Democratic one instead the notion any of the other four ones may be preferable to it is selfevidently massively flawed in my view

Sounds chill but Day 3 would start with whatever is most important to you British Museum gets absolutely rammed so have a plan of what you want to see beforehand

I would take the uber boat from the Tower of London to Westminster Its a lovely part of the river to be on and you see london in a different way Then you can explore the Westminster area A nice loop from the pier is 1 Houses of Parliament and Big Ben 2 Westminster Abbey 3 St Jamess Park 4 Buckingham Palace 5 Down The Mall to Trafalgar Square 6 Portrait Gallery optional if you have time but its free and they have toilets and a nice cafe 7 Down Whitehall passing Downing Street Horseguards and the Cenotaph 8 back to the start

Day 2 Walking from the Tower to Westminster is doable and it takes around 4060 minutes to do so still if I were you I would take a bus to have a bit of rest between those locations you will walk a lot anyway The views are also nice I would say more diverse than if you go along the river

Generally this sounds like a really fun itinerary Some things to be aware of The stalls at Camden Market are shut in the evening but its a buzzy area with food bars etc Fun alternative vibes still despite losing some of its famous countercultural feel Some of the street food places are Insta famous so are a little expensive for what they are Day 3 probably too much here You could probably visit the Hunterian tour only I think or the British Museum Both might feel rushed Would potentially recommend choosing a part of the British Museum that particularly interests you and focusing on it rather than trying to take all of it in this makes things less memorable and the crowds can really be a lot Both Hunterian and British Museum are about 15mins walk from Cecil CourtNeils Yard so would be good places for a browse afterwards

Suggestionafter Tower of London go across Tower Bridge turn rightwalk along the Southbankso much to see can go through Bourough Market for a snack pass Tate Modern and Globe theater walk under the Eye great photos and amazing photos of Westminister and Elizabeth TowerBig Ben from this vantage pointcross Westminister Bridge for a closer look about of Parliament and Westministerthere is a sculpture garden you will see also of important people in history Neals Yard is cool quaint but a been and done in minutesgreat photo momentacross from it is Seven Dials marketgreat for a bite to eat lovely shops in the area and theaters I dont think its too much We love London and easily did 2530000 steps a day

You can move Neils Yard and Covent Garden to day 2 late afternoonevening With the right route you could get Cecil Court in before the shops shut note some shut very early if you were actually planning on going inside If just a walk down the outside then anytime is fine obviously Day 3 Again Do Hampstead later afternoonevening unless you actually plan on clothes shopping The Heath doesnt shut so you can do a later afternoon walk or early evening walk All the pubs and restaurants in Hampstead are open late Or if youre an early bird you can do a Health walk at something like 6630am pop to a cafe in South End Green or Hampstead Village for breakfast and then head to the British Museum
If you are into pubs there are some great ones in Hampstead I highly recommend the Old White Bear on New End Square and the King william VI on the high street

seems fine Remember that Camden Market is not open for stalls and shops in the evening there will be some drinks places open though Maybe do this on sunday If you are adventurous you can walk to the stadium or a bus
